executive_summary.one_paragraph: SRC (P12931) is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase anchored exclusively to the cytoplasmic inner leaflet of the plasma membrane via N-myristoylation; it has no extracellular domain and no transmembrane segment. All evidence—biophysical inner-leaflet anchoring, membrane-fractionation MS, HPA IHC—demonstrates plasma-membrane association but not extracellular-face accessibility. SURFY/CSPA exclude SRC from the surfaceome. Approved drugs (dasatinib, bosutinib) act intracellularly. Surface accessibility is therefore low; any engagement strategy requires cell-permeable or intrabody-type binders.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: SRC (P12931) is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase anchored to the cytoplasmic inner leaflet of the plasma membrane via N-myristoylation at Gly-2, not a canonical surface-accessible protein with an extracellular domain. HPA IHC (supported reliability) detects SRC at plasma membrane and cell junctions; multiple membrane-fractionation and surfaceome MS studies co-isolate SRC with membrane-enriched fractions (SKBR3 membrane proteome, NIS-PM complex pull-down, chondrogenic surfaceome); and biophysical studies confirm lipid-bilayer anchoring via the myristoylated SH4 domain. However, no direct live-cell flow cytometry or cell-surface biotinylation study demonstrates extracellular-face accessibility of SRC. SURFY and CSPA databases do not vote SRC as a surface protein. The strongest evidence category is plasma membrane localization by IHC and membrane proteomics fractionation, both indirect for surface accessibility.

expression_observations.context: HPA IHC detects SRC at plasma membrane, cell junctions, and vesicles across multiple tissue types; reliability tier: supported
SRC identified in cell-membrane proteome of SKBR3 HER2+ breast cancer cells by membrane-enrichment LC-MS/MS (Karcini & Lazar 2022)
SRC kinase detected in surfaceome cluster 2 of chondrogenic cells by sialoglycoprotein-enrichment LC-MS/MS (Kovacs et al. 2025)
SRC co-isolated with NIS plasma membrane complexes using intact-cell labeling and immunoprecipitation in thyroid cancer cells (Faria et al. 2021)
Biophysical studies confirm myristoylated Src anchors to lipid bilayer inner leaflet via SH4 domain and forms condensates (Mohammad et al. 2025)

non_surface_expression.context: IHC in breast carcinoma cell lines shows activated c-Src predominantly in the cytoplasm, indicating a large intracellular pool (Ito et al. 2002)
c-Src localizes to endosomal membranes in transformed cells and is encapsulated in secreted exosomes, revealing a non-plasma-membrane pool (Hikita et al. 2019)
UniProt records multiple subcellular locations for SRC including cytoplasm, perinuclear region, nucleus, and mitochondrion inner membrane, confirming substantial intracellular pools

therapeutic_engagement.description: Dasatinib (Sprycel) and bosutinib are FDA-approved BCR-ABL/SRC kinase inhibitors used in CML and ALL. These drugs bind intracellularly to the ATP-binding pocket of SRC's kinase domain and do not engage an extracellular epitope.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: All approved SRC-targeting drugs act on the intracellular kinase domain. SRC has no extracellular domain; no antibody therapeutic targeting a surface-exposed SRC epitope is currently approved.

contradicting_evidence.claim: SURFY and CSPA databases do not classify SRC as a cell-surface protein, consistent with its lack of a transmembrane domain and extracellular-facing epitopes.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: SRC is a peripheral membrane protein anchored to the cytoplasmic inner leaflet via N-myristoylation. It has no TM domain or ECD and is not accessible from the extracellular space. Surfaceome databases using impermeant-label strategies correctly exclude it.

contradicting_evidence.claim: Immunofluorescence in breast carcinoma cell lines shows activated c-Src predominantly in the cytoplasm, not at the plasma membrane surface.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: SRC dynamically partitions between the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane and endomembranes. Cytoplasmic staining reflects physiological cycling rather than absence from the membrane.

cell_states.descriptor: Prolonged oxLDL or free cholesterol exposure reprograms monocytes into a persistent pro-inflammatory phenotype with membrane clustering of Src driving sustained activation of the Src-SYK-mTORC1-STAT3/5 axis.
Fluid shear stress activates c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes membrane-proximal redistribution in osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells.
In c-Src-transformed cells, activated Src redistributes to endosomal membranes and promotes exosome secretion; plasma-membrane Src is diminished relative to endosomal pool.
SRC kinase is detected in the surfaceome of chondrogenic cells specifically at day 6 of differentiation, when kinase-activity-linked GO terms peak.

dual_localization.condition: adhesion-dependent; co-localises with integrin signalling complexes

membrane_subdomains.subdomain: cell junctions (HPA: junctional localization, Supported reliability)

anatomical_accessibility.context: Systemic or intravascular access (blood vessels, tumors)
anatomical_accessibility.rationale: SRC is a cytoplasmic-face peripheral membrane kinase anchored by N-myristoylation with no extracellular domain. Its kinase domain faces the cytoplasm, making it inaccessible to extracellular antibodies or ligands under normal membrane integrity; access requires delivery of cell-permeable or intrabody-type binders.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Quiescent cells; Src in closed/inactive conformation, distributed between cytosol and plasma membrane inner leaflet
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Activating phosphorylation at Tyr419 (autocatalysis) or dephosphorylation of inhibitory Tyr530; loss of CSK-mediated suppression
accessibility_modulation.change: Conformational opening of Src exposes kinase domain; membrane association increases via N-myristoylation and SH4 lysine cluster; lipid-driven condensates form at inner plasma membrane leaflet.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Inner-leaflet Src becomes more stably membrane-anchored when activated, but remains inaccessible to extracellular binders; relevant for intrabody or lipid-raft-targeting strategies.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Unstimulated osteoblast-like cells with low c-Src Tyr416 phosphorylation and cytoplasmic/peripheral Src distribution
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Fluid shear stress applied to osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells
accessibility_modulation.change: Fluid shear stress increases c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes co-localisation of Src with RANKL at the cell periphery and membrane fraction.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Mechanically activated Src accumulates at the plasma membrane inner leaflet in osteoblasts; this is relevant for bone biology and mechanical-loading contexts but Src itself remains cytoplasmic-face.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting monocytes with basal Src membrane association
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Sustained oxLDL or free cholesterol challenge (monocytes)
accessibility_modulation.change: Membrane clustering of Src drives the Src-SYK-mTORC1-STAT3/5 signalling axis; inflammatory state persists even after stimulus withdrawal (immune memory-like phenotype). Fumagillin (myristoylation inhibitor) disrupts Src clustering and alleviates activation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Src membrane clustering is myristoylation-dependent; inhibiting N-myristoylation dissociates Src from membrane and ablates the sustained inflammatory signal in monocytes.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal epithelial or fibroblast cells with predominantly plasma-membrane-associated Src
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Oncogenic transformation / c-Src overexpression in cancer cells
accessibility_modulation.change: Activated c-Src redistributes to endosomal membranes, where it promotes ESCRT-mediated intraluminal vesicle formation and exosome secretion; plasma-membrane pool is supplemented by an endosomal pool.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In Src-upregulated cancers a significant fraction of active Src resides on endosomal membranes rather than the plasma membrane, which impacts targeting strategies that rely on cell-surface accessibility.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Early-stage or late-stage chondrogenic differentiation (days 1–3 or days 10–15 of micromass culture)
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC kinase expression in the surfaceome peaks at day 6, the stage at which kinase activity, ephrin-receptor and FGF-signalling GO terms are most enriched; expression subsides thereafter.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surfaceome-level SRC is transiently elevated at a specific differentiation checkpoint in cartilage development; this stage-specific window is relevant for targeting chondrogenic precursors.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Plasma membrane inner leaflet (primary compartment, N-myristoylation-anchored)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Transformed / cancer cells with high Src activity
accessibility_modulation.change: c-Src distributes between plasma membrane and endosomal membrane; endosomal pool is activated and encapsulated in secreted exosomes.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: The split between plasma-membrane and endosomal pools means that total cellular Src is not synonymous with cell-surface Src; the endosomal fraction is non-accessible to extracellular binders.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (whole-protein pLDDT — no ECD residues in topology)

surface_bind.attribution: © Marchand, Khakzad, Correia et al. — EPFL / Inria / Novo Nordisk

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: SRC membrane anchoring is entirely myristoylation-driven (Gly2 N-myristoylation + SH4 lysine cluster); no obligate co-receptor is required for membrane association. The NIS/SRC interaction reflects a regulatory complex at the PM inner leaflet rather than a trafficking dependency.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: SRC is restricted to the cytoplasmic (inner) leaflet of the plasma membrane; it is not accessible from the extracellular face. Within the inner leaflet it further concentrates in lipid-raft-like condensates, focal adhesions, and cell junctions depending on activation state, further limiting binder access strategies.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: SRC is a peripheral membrane protein with no transmembrane domain and no extracellular domain; its entire kinase, SH2, and SH3 domains face the cytoplasm. Biophysical studies confirm exclusive inner-leaflet anchoring via the myristoylated SH4 domain. No extracellular epitope exists for antibody or biologics engagement.

epitope_masking.rationale: Epitope masking is not applicable: SRC has no extracellular domain and therefore no extracellular epitope to be masked. All functional domains are intracellular and inaccessible to conventional biologics without cell penetration.

confidence_reasoning: A1 grades evidence as 'supportive_but_indirect' and this synthesis tracks that grade. The topology is unambiguous—inner-leaflet peripheral kinase, no ECD—supported by strong biophysical primary data (a1_evi_09, a1_evi_10, a2_evi_12) and consistent database exclusion from surfaceome calls (a1_evi_14). However, multiple membrane-fractionation MS studies superficially detect SRC in surface fractions, creating mild conflicting signal. State-dependence is moderate: PM association is modulated by activation state, oncogenic transformation (endosomal redistribution), and differentiation stage (chondrogenic day-6 peak). Confidence is 'moderate' rather than 'high' because the surface-fraction MS detections require careful interpretation and state-dependent variability is real.
